name: Adapa
description: Adapa is a prominent figure in Mesopotamian mythology, primarily known for his encounter with Anu, the sky god. He is often depicted as a sage or wise man, a priest of Ea (Enki), the god of wisdom and fresh water, from the city of Eridu. The myth of Adapa explores themes of mortality, divine gifts, and the ambiguous nature of knowledge. Adapa's story revolves around his being summoned to the heavens after breaking the wing of the South Wind. Ea, his patron deity, advises him to refuse the food and water of death offered by Anu, as they would ensure his immortality. However, Anu, impressed by Adapa's wisdom and humility, instead offers him the food and water of life. Ironically, Adapa, following Ea's instructions, refuses the offering, thereby forfeiting immortality for humankind. His tale serves as an etiology for human mortality and highlights the complex relationship between gods and humans in Mesopotamian thought. Some interpretations suggest that Adapa's actions were not necessarily a failure, but rather a fulfillment of a predetermined divine plan, maintaining the natural order.
subtitle: The Wise Man of Eridu

keyMyths.title: Adapa and the South Wind
keyMyths.description: Adapa, while fishing in the sea, is angered by the South Wind which capsizes his boat and prevents him from fishing. In a fit of rage, he breaks the wing of the South Wind, rendering it unable to blow for seven days. This disruption of the natural order draws the attention of Anu, the sky god, who summons Adapa to heaven to answer for his actions. The myth explores the consequences of human actions affecting the divine realm and highlights Adapa's extraordinary power.
keyMyths.source: ANET (Ancient Near Eastern Texts Relating to the Old Testament), Pritchard, James B., ed.

keyMyths.title: The Advice of Ea
keyMyths.description: Before ascending to heaven, Adapa is given specific instructions by Ea, his patron deity. Ea warns Adapa that Anu will offer him food and water of death and instructs him to refuse them, thereby avoiding the fate of mortality. However, Ea's motives are ambiguous; he may be protecting Adapa, or he may be ensuring that humanity remains mortal. This episode showcases the complex and often self-serving relationships between gods and humans in Mesopotamian belief.
keyMyths.source: Dalley, Stephanie. Myths from Mesopotamia: Creation, The Flood, Gilgamesh, and Others.

keyMyths.title: The Refusal of Immortality
keyMyths.description: Upon arriving in heaven, Adapa is treated with respect and offered the food and water of life by Anu. However, adhering to Ea's instructions, Adapa refuses the offering, believing it to be poisoned. Anu, realizing the deception, laments Adapa's decision, stating that he has forfeited immortality for himself and, by extension, for humankind. This act solidifies the mortal condition of humanity and raises questions about the true nature of divine benevolence.
keyMyths.source: Black, Jeremy, and Anthony Green. Gods, Demons and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia.
